front: Work: this book's many lists include 'Hateful Things,' 'Embarrassing Things,' 'Pleasing Things,' and 'Adorable Things'
work: The Pillow Book (Lists and Zuihitsu Genre)

front: Work: this book belongs to the zuihitsu genre ('follow the brush'), alongside An Account of My Hut and Essays in Idleness
work: The Pillow Book (Lists and Zuihitsu Genre)

front: Work: 'Things That Make One's Heart Beat Faster' in this book includes 'sparrows feeding their young'
work: The Pillow Book (Lists and Zuihitsu Genre)

front: Work: 'Things That Should Be Large' includes 'Priests. Fruit. Houses.' in this book
work: The Pillow Book (Lists and Zuihitsu Genre)

front: Work: 'a lying-in room when the baby has died' is listed as a 'Dispiriting Thing' in this book
work: The Pillow Book (Lists and Zuihitsu Genre)

front: Work: this book opens with 'In spring, the dawn...' linking seasons to the best time of day
Work: the key concept 'okashi' (amusing/delightful) in this book describes fireflies and rain in its opening passage
Work: a dog beaten nearly to death for attacking a popular cat later receives an Imperial pardon when it whimpers
work: The Pillow Book (Anecdotes and Court Life)

front: Work: the narrator bets on how long a mound of snow will last and hires a gardener to protect it
work: The Pillow Book (Anecdotes and Court Life)

front: Work: the narrator flirts with the courtier Yukinari (Controller First Secretary), discussing a false cock's crow at a barrier
work: The Pillow Book (Anecdotes and Court Life)

front: Work: the 'defense of the career girl' section of this book is used by Edith Serra as the basis for a 'Poetics of Voyeurism'
work: The Pillow Book (Anecdotes and Court Life)

front: Work: the narrator explains a toponym with a story about a captain who threads an ant through a jewel to prevent an invasion
work: The Pillow Book (Anecdotes and Court Life)

front: Author: Murasaki Shikibu called this author 'dreadfully conceited' for littering her writing with Chinese characters
Author: Ruth Ozeki's My Year of Meats frequently alludes to this author's book; it inspires Jane Takagi-Little's documentaries
Author: in Anita Desai's Fire on the Mountain, Nanda Kaul reflects on this book's claim that women who live alone should have dilapidated houses
Work: a parody called the 'Dog' version of this book was published alongside 'Mad Verses'
work: The Pillow Book (Anecdotes and Court Life)
